howItWorks.intro: This setlist time calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Total Time = (Songs × Avg Length) + ((Songs-1) × Gap) + Banter
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: How long should a set be?
faq.answer: Club sets are typically 45-60 minutes. Bar gigs often run 3-4 hours with 3-4 sets and breaks. Festival slots vary from 30-90 minutes.

faq.question: Is the Setlist Time Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard music and audio formulas trusted by musicians and audio engineers. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What number of songs should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate number of songs value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Setlist Time Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

sections.title: What Is the Setlist Time Calculator?
sections.body: The Setlist Time Calculator is a free online tool designed for musicians and audio engineers who need quick, accurate calculations in the music and audio space. By entering your number of songs, avg song length, between songs, you get instant results including total set time, music time, dead time. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting total set time right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In music and audio, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by musicians and audio engineers worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to fine-tune your sound with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your number of songs and need to find the right total set time. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Number of Songs
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your number of songs — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: avg song length, between songs, banter/tuning. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your total set time, music time, dead time. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your number of songs before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both total set time and music time — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to fine-tune your sound.
If you're unsure about your banter/tuning, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
